content: What is the correct procedure for filing an FIR?

To file a First Information Report (FIR) correctly under Section 154 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC), follow these steps:

1. Visit or contact your nearest police station.
2. Inform them that you wish to register an FIR against someone/somebody in relation to certain offences as defined by law, such as those listed under Sections 302 and beyond PPC for murder cases.

Under Section 154(3) of the CrPC:
- The officer-in-charge (OIC), upon receiving information about a cognizable offence that is not trivial or frivolous but has sufficient grounds warranting investigation should register an FIR.
  
If you are unable to visit due to circumstances such as being in custody, follow these steps:

1. Send your complaint via registered post with acknowledgment due addressed either directly at the police station concerned (if known) and/or sent through a legal representative or advocate.

Under Section 154(4)(b):
- If an FIR is not filed within seven days of receiving information about cognizable offences by any officer-in-charge, it can be done so after fourteen days from receipt thereof in writing to:
   - The Superintendent of Police (SP) at the police station where such offence was committed.
   - Or if no SP has been appointed for that area or is not available within seven days and thereafter up to 90 days.

2. If you are unable, due to circumstances beyond your control like being in a foreign country without access to communication facilities with Pakistan, send an FIR through registered post addressed at the Superintendent of Police (SP) concerned as per Section 154(4)(b).

3. In case there is no SP available or if it cannot be reached within seven days and thereafter up to ninety days from receipt thereof in writing by a person authorized on behalf of any other police officer, you may send an FIR through registered post addressed at the Superintendent General (SGP) concerned as per Section 154(4)(c).

Remember that once filed with your nearest station or sent via postal service under CrPC provisions to higher authorities like SP/SGP if necessary within specified time frames. The investigating authority will then proceed accordingly based on this FIR.

Note: Always keep a copy of the FIR and any acknowledgment received for future reference, as it is crucial evidence in criminal proceedings.
Explain to me how to properly handle the property issue. I just want what is rightfully mine.

To address your concern regarding handling an alleged wrongful possession or encroachment on your rightful property under Pakistani law:

1. Gather Evidence: Collect all relevant documents that prove ownership of the disputed land, such as title deeds and any other legal papers.

2. Legal Notice (if applicable): Send a formal notice to the person currently in possession demanding them to vacate within an agreed-upon time frame or face legal action under Section 144 CrPC for forcible entry/frolic upon property if they refuse voluntarily after receiving your demand letter, as per Sections 145 and beyond of PPC.

3. File FIR: If there is no voluntary compliance with the notice sent by you (or through a lawyer), file an FIR at the nearest police station against wrongful possession or encroachment under Section 452 CrPC for theft if it involves taking away your property unlawfully, as per Sections 451 and beyond of PPC.

4. Civil Suit: Simultaneously consider filing a civil suit in court to recover ownership rights (under Order XXXII Rule 1A-2 CPC) against the person currently holding possession or encroaching upon land that is rightfully yours under Section 5 CrPC for recovery suits, as per Sections 6 and beyond of PPC.

5. Injunction: Seek an injunction order from a court to restrain any further unlawful occupation/encroachment on your property until final adjudication by the courts (Order XXXII Rule 1 CPC).

Remember that it is advisable always to consult with or hire legal counsel experienced in Pakistani criminal law and civil procedure for guidance through this process. They can help you navigate complexities, draft necessary documents correctly such as FIRs/complaints/civil suits notices/injunction applications etc., represent your interests effectively before the court/judge(s), ensure all procedural requirements are met timely to avoid unnecessary delays or complications.

Note: The above steps provide a general guideline based on Pakistani law. However, specific circumstances may require tailored legal advice and action for effective resolution of property disputes under Pakistan's criminal procedure laws (CrPC) as well as civil procedures in the courts.
A lawyer is bound to what extent according to law? Like what he is bound to do and what should i expect from him?

Under Section 126 CrPC, a practicing advocate or an attorney-at-law shall not be compelled by any court of competent jurisdiction at any stage after commencement of proceedings in the case before it (except as provided under Sections 127-129) for disclosure against himself/himself and his client:

1. Confidential communications between him/themself with clients.
2. Information relating to legal advice given or received from a professional point of view, unless such communication is made without confidence.

However, an advocate may be compelled by the court under Section 127 CrPC if it concerns any matter in relation to which he/she has been engaged as counsel for one party and not disclosed during his/her engagement. In this case:

- The lawyer must disclose information that was previously undisclosed but now relevant.
  
Under Sections 128 & beyond, an advocate may be compelled by the court under Section 129 CrPC if it concerns any matter in relation to which he/she has been engaged as counsel for one party and disclosed during his/her engagement. In this case:

- The lawyer must disclose information that was previously undisclosed but now relevant.

In summary:
1. An attorney is bound not to reveal confidential communications or legal advice between himself/herself/themselves with clients.
2. He/she may be compelled by the court under Section 127 CrPC if he/she has been engaged as counsel for one party and failed to disclose certain information during his/her engagement that now becomes relevant in a case before it (except where such communication was made without confidence).
3. An attorney can also reveal confidential communications or legal advice between himself/herself/themselves with clients under Section 129 CrPC if he/she has been engaged as counsel for one party and disclosed certain information during his/her engagement that now becomes relevant in a case before it.

As an advocate/client, you should expect your lawyer to maintain confidentiality regarding all discussions unless otherwise agreed upon. You can also discuss any concerns or expectations with him/themself directly at the outset of representation.
  
Note: The above steps provide general guidelines based on Pakistani law (CrPC). However, specific circumstances may require tailored legal advice and action for effective resolution under Pakistan's criminal procedure laws as well as civil procedures in courts.
